risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization was conducted using standard opaque envelopes with foil (to prevent seeing the group assignment inside the envelope) and carbon paper (to provide an audit trail). The envelopes were randomly sorted by an individual not involved with the randomization visit process, then numbered sequentially... Randomization was carried out using sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes according to standard protocols [29], with randomization stratified by the 2 cohorts to promote adequate numbers of participants able to talk to one another through the app." Comment: The study describes a structured randomization procedure using sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes and stratification by cohort, which are consistent with proper random allocation. Although the exact generator is not stated, the reporting is sufficient to support a true random sequence rather than a non-random rule.

Quote: "Randomization was conducted using standard opaque envelopes with foil (to prevent seeing the group assignment inside the envelope) and carbon paper (to provide an audit trail). The envelopes were randomly sorted by an individual not involved with the randomization visit process, then numbered sequentially. ... Randomization was carried out using sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes according to standard protocols" Comment: This explicitly describes a sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelope system that would prevent the recruiter from foreseeing the next allocation before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants could not be blinded to their group." Comment: The intervention was a wearable activity monitor plus tablet and weekly counseling versus wait-list control, so both participants and personnel delivering the intervention knew allocation. Because the outcomes included behavior-related measures (physical activity/sedentary behavior) and other potentially influenceable outcomes such as body fat and fitness, knowledge of group assignment could plausibly affect performance and outcome-related behavior, so the study is at high risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "Physical activity was measured using an ActivPAL device..."; "Fitness was estimated using a 6-min walk test... with a trained assessor tracking the time and laps completed"; "Percent body fat was estimated using Dual x-ray Absorptiometry (GE Lunar iDXA...)"; "Participants could not be blinded to their group. Unfortunately, resource limitations precluded using blinded assessors for all participants." Comment: The main reported outcomes were measured with objective devices (ActivPAL, DEXA) and standardized performance testing, so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced measurement of these outcomes in a meaningful way. Although the paper states that blinded assessors were not used for all participants, the objective nature of the primary and key secondary outcomes supports a low risk judgement for detection bias.

Quote: "In the intervention group, 1 participant dropped out as compared with 1 in the wait list control." and "All the analyses used the intent-to-treat principle, bringing the last observation forward for the ones who dropped out." Comment: Attrition was minimal and balanced across groups (1/20 in each arm, i.e. 5% per arm), with no indication that dropout was related to lack of efficacy or adverse events; the study also used an ITT analysis with LOCF, which is acceptable here given the very low and balanced missingness.
